summary: Tawhid al-rububiyya (the oneness of lordship) is the affirmation that God alone is the Creator, Sustainer, Owner, and Disposer of all things. In the threefold division of tawhid characteristic of the Najdi/Wahhabi and broader Salafi-Athari tradition, it is the first category, distinguished from the oneness of worship (tawhid al-uluhiyya) and the oneness of the divine names and attributes (tawhid al-asma' wa-l-sifat).
description: Tawhid al-rububiyya (from rabb, 'lord' or 'master', hence 'the oneness of lordship') is the affirmation that God alone creates, sustains, provides, gives life and death, and governs the cosmos, with no partner in His lordship over creation. In the threefold scheme of tawhid that became characteristic of the modern Salafi-Athari tradition, tawhid is divided into three interrelated categories: tawhid al-rububiyya (the oneness of lordship), tawhid al-uluhiyya (the oneness of worship, that God alone is to be worshipped), and tawhid al-asma' wa-l-sifat (the oneness of the divine names and attributes, affirmed as revealed without figurative reinterpretation or likening to creatures). On this framing, the first category, lordship, is held to be a matter that even those the tradition regards as idolaters (the polytheists addressed by the Qur'an) acknowledged, so that acknowledgement of God's lordship is presented as necessary but not by itself sufficient for true monotheism: the decisive test is whether worship too is reserved for God alone (tawhid al-uluhiyya). Contemporary Salafi exposition (for example Salih al-Fawzan's commentary on Ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab's Kitab al-Tawhid) presents the three categories as a systematic articulation of the creed drawn from the Qur'an and Sunnah, in which affirming God's exclusive lordship is the ground upon which the reservation of worship to Him is built. The substance of the rububiyya/uluhiyya distinction is drawn from Ibn Taymiyya (d. 728/1328), who argued that the Qur'an's central concern is the devotion of worship to God alone, and who held that the polytheists of Mecca affirmed God's lordship yet violated His exclusive right to be worshipped; modern scholarship (Jon Hoover) reads Ibn Taymiyya's own account as a twofold distinction (rububiyya and uluhiyya) that the later Najdi and Salafi tradition systematized into the fixed threefold division, with the third named category commonly credited to Ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab. The threefold scheme is presented here as the framing of the Salafi-Athari tradition on its own terms and by its own reception; other Sunni theological currents (Ash'ari and Maturidi) did not all organize tawhid in this way, and some regard the rububiyya/uluhiyya division as a later systematization or contest its polemical deployment. Those disagreements are named rather than settled.
editorial_notes: Per neutrality principles #1, #2, and #7, this entry presents the threefold division of tawhid, and tawhid al-rububiyya's place within it, as the Salafi-Athari tradition's own framing and reception, attributed to it as its position, rather than as the settled structure of Islamic theology as such. The tradition's self-understanding is represented fully and on its own terms: that acknowledging God's exclusive lordship is necessary but not sufficient, and that the decisive test of monotheism is reserving worship for God alone. That self-understanding, and the framing that even the Qur'anic polytheists affirmed God's lordship, are reported as the tradition's attributed position, not endorsed. Correspondingly, the fact that other Sunni schools (Ash'ari, Maturidi) do not all adopt the threefold scheme, and that some scholars and other-school voices regard the rububiyya/uluhiyya division as a later systematization, is named without adjudication; the entry does not rule that the division is correct or that its critics are correct. The attribution of the fixed threefold scheme to the later Najdi/Salafi reception, with the substance of the rububiyya/uluhiyya distinction drawn from Ibn Taymiyya and the third category commonly credited to Ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab (following Hoover), is kept consistent with the tawhid-al-uluhiyya, ibn-taymiyya, and muhammad-ibn-abd-al-wahhab entries in the same corpus. Relation targets tawhid-al-uluhiyya, tawhid, ibn-taymiyya, and al-asma-wa-l-sifat are all existing entries; the third category of the threefold scheme (the oneness of the divine names and attributes) is linked to the existing al-asma-wa-l-sifat concept, the broader names-and-attributes topic under which that Salafi-Athari category falls, rather than to a separate node. Per neutrality principle #8 the sources triangulate three lenses: a peer-reviewed academic chapter on Ibn Taymiyya's account of the types of tawhid (Hoover, 'The Tawhids of Ibn Taymiyya', AMI Press 2025); the tradition's primary texts (Ibn Taymiyya on the two tawhids and Ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab's Kitab al-Tawhid); and a contemporary Salafi scholarly voice presenting the threefold doctrine on its own terms (Salih al-Fawzan's Concise Commentary on the Book of Tawhid). The Hoover chapter preview was consulted directly for the threefold-tawhid genealogy and carries high confidence for that claim, consistent with the ibn-taymiyya entry; the two primary texts and the al-Fawzan commentary were web-verified as existing and on-topic but not read line by line, so they are medium.

relations.note: The two are distinguished within the threefold scheme: the tradition holds that acknowledging God as sole Lord (rububiyya) does not by itself make one a true monotheist unless worship too is reserved for God alone (uluhiyya). On this framing, affirming lordship is presented as necessary but not sufficient.
Distinguished from and paired with the oneness of worship as the first and second categories of the Salafi-Athari threefold division of tawhid.

The third category of the threefold scheme is the oneness of the divine names and attributes (tawhid al-asma' wa-l-sifat), affirmed as revealed without figurative reinterpretation and commonly credited to Ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab. It is linked here to the existing concept of the divine names and attributes (al-asma-wa-l-sifat), the broader topic under which the Salafi-Athari category falls.
One of the three components of tawhid in the Salafi/Wahhabi threefold division, alongside tawhid al-uluhiyya and tawhid al-asma' wa-l-sifat.

The substance of the rububiyya/uluhiyya distinction is drawn from Ibn Taymiyya (d. 728/1328), whose account modern scholarship reads as a twofold distinction later systematized into the fixed threefold division by the Najdi/Salafi tradition.

historical_period.note: The substance of the rububiyya/uluhiyya distinction is medieval, elaborated by Ibn Taymiyya (d. 728/1328); the fixed threefold scheme within which tawhid al-rububiyya is the first named category is associated with Muhammad ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab's Kitab al-Tawhid (mid-18th c.) and the later Salafi reception, and remains central to that tradition today. Start year is approximate and marks Ibn Taymiyya's activity.

sources.citation: Hoover, Jon. 'The Tawhids of Ibn Taymiyya.' In Islamic Perspectives on God and (Other) Monotheism(s), edited by Wahid M. Amin, Aaron W. Hughes, and Sajjad H. Rizvi, 89-110. Birmingham: AMI Press, 2025 (ISBN 978-1915550057). A peer-reviewed academic chapter analysing Ibn Taymiyya's twofold account of tawhid (tawhid al-rububiyya, the oneness of lordship, and tawhid al-uluhiyya, the exclusive right of God to be worshipped) and its relation to the later threefold division; used as the modern scholarly lens on the doctrine and its genealogy. The chapter preview was consulted directly for the threefold-tawhid genealogy (Ibn Taymiyya's twofold rububiyya/uluhiyya distinction, later systematized into the fixed threefold division, with the third category commonly credited to Ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab), which it supports; this is the same reading, at high confidence, that the ibn-taymiyya entry carries for this source.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, text not reproduced.

sources.citation: Ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab, Muhammad (d. 1206/1792). Kitab al-Tawhid (The Book of the Oneness of God), 12th/18th c. The foundational treatise of the Najdi revival, which treats the reservation of worship to God alone (tawhid al-uluhiyya) as the decisive test of monotheism, on the ground that acknowledgement of God's lordship (tawhid al-rububiyya) does not by itself suffice. Classical primary source; not read line by line for this draft.
sources.license: Primary source; cited for reference, not reproduced.

sources.citation: al-Fawzan, Salih ibn Fawzan. Concise Commentary on the Book of Tawhid (a commentary on Muhammad ibn 'Abd al-Wahhab's Kitab al-Tawhid), trans. from the Arabic. Riyadh: Al-Maiman Publishing House, 2009. A contemporary Salafi scholar's systematic exposition presenting the threefold division of tawhid (rububiyya, uluhiyya, asma' wa-l-sifat) on its own terms, in which affirming God's exclusive lordship is the ground for reserving worship to Him; the contemporary Salafi-scholarly lens. Relevant passages not read line-by-line for this draft.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, text not reproduced.
